images.caption: Figure 1:Left.Ranking models on held-out challenging reasoning tasks (as measured by mean CV Spearmanρ\rho) using our linear RankSVM proxy. Our proxy uses features of the next-token prediction distributions of candidate models over expert reasoning traces.Right:Ranking 25 pretraining corpora for a target 1B LLM on the DataDecide testbed(Magnussonet al.,2025). Each method trains small proxy models (4M–90M) on each corpus and attempts to recover the ground-truth corpus ranking defined by the target 1B model’s downstream accuracy. Decision accuracy is the fraction of corpus pairs ranked correctly. Our best proxy metric pushes the Pareto frontier, requiring roughly10,000×10{,}000\timesless compute to match the downstream evaluation baseline.
Figure 2:An illustration of our method. We use a candidate model’s next-token prediction distribution at each token of an expert’s trajectory to calculate our proxy metrics.

header: 4Cross-Family Model Selection: Ranking LLMs on Unseen Tasks
5Pretraining Data Selection: Ranking Datasets with Smaller LMs

images.caption: Figure 3:Extrapolating proxy metrics along the training trajectory.Left:pretraining checkpoints of OLMo-3-7B on four reasoning benchmarks.Right:post-training checkpoints of OLMo-3-7B-Think on four reasoning benchmarks. Filled markers are the training window, stars are held-out checkpoints, solid curves are power-law fits from the training window, and dashed curves are extrapolations. The plots for other benchmarks are provided inFigures˜10and11in the Appendix.
Table 3:Extrapolation RMSE for predicting downstream accuracy at1.41.4M steps from checkpoints up to8080K steps (∼18×{\sim}18\times).Boldmarks the lowest RMSE per task.

Figure 4:Extrapolating HellaSwag accuracy along the OLMo-3-7B pretraining trajectory. The proxy power-law fit (RMSE=0.003=0.003) tracks the target far more closely than the CE loss exponential (RMSE=0.09=0.09).

Figure 5:Proxy metric selection frequency (normalized) for univariate (left) and 3-sparse proxy (right). Darker cells are chosen more often. In both cases, a small cluster of proxy metrics accounts for almost all selections. The bulk of the 80 proxy metrics are unused.

Figure 6:Likelihood-based baselines against a learned proxy.Left:the three loss-based baselines (FineWeb cross-entropy loss, uniform expert-trajectory cross-entropy loss, and rBridge) plotted against MMLU-Pro accuracy across 18 language models. Low loss is a weak and non-monotonic indicator of downstream ranking across model families and post-training recipes.Right:the learned RankSVM (linear) proxy, evaluated on MMLU-Pro in a fold where MMLU-Pro was held out, produces a nearly monotonic relationship with downstream accuracy. The same proxy was not exposed to MMLU-Pro during fitting.
Figure 7:Performance of the linear RankSVM proxy as we vary the number of held-out tasks and the fraction of models used for selection. Each cell reports the mean Spearmanρ\rhoon held-out tasks.
Figure 8:Performance of the 3-sparse proxy as we vary the number of held-out tasks and the fraction of models used for selection. Each cell reports the mean Spearmanρ\rhoon held-out tasks.

Figure 9:Ranking LLMs with the33-sparse proxy.Downstream accuracy vs. proxy score for each of the six benchmarks on a randomly sampled held-out fold. Same format asFigure˜1(left) but using the33-sparse proxy instead of the linear RankSVM.

Figure 10:Pretraining extrapolation on AIME and SuperGPQA.Same protocol asFigure˜3(left) for the two benchmarks not shown in the main figure. The power-law fits from the training window (filled markers) extrapolate to the held-out checkpoint (star) on both tasks.

Figure 11:Post-training extrapolation on AIME.Same protocol asFigure˜3(right). The proxy metric evolves monotonically with post-training step and the power-law fit extrapolates to the held-out checkpoint.

Figure 12:Proxy metric vs. downstream accuracy at post-training checkpoints.The best selected univariate proxy is plotted against downstream accuracy on USACO (left) and HMMT (right) across post-training checkpoints of OLMo-3-7B-Think. The strong monotonic relationship confirms that the extrapolated proxy tracks the ranking of interest.

Figure 13:Direct sigmoid extrapolation of HellaSwag accuracy.Accuracy is fit as a sigmoid oflog10⁡(steps)\log_{10}(\text{steps})followingOwen (2024). Circles are the training window (up to8080K steps), the star is the held-out checkpoint at1.41.4M steps. The fit overshoots the held-out accuracy (RMSE=0.11=0.11).

Figure 14:Extrapolating Winogrande accuracy along the pretraining trajectory of OLMo-3-7B.Circles are the training window (up to8080K steps), the star is the held-out checkpoint at1.41.4M steps (∼18×{\sim}18\timesthe training compute).Left:accuracy vs.log10⁡(steps)\log_{10}(\text{steps}), fit with a sigmoid (RMSE=0.02=0.02).Centre:accuracy vs. CE loss on FineWeb, fit with an exponential (RMSE=0.08=0.08).Right:accuracy vs. the best univariate proxy, fit with a power law (RMSE=0.01=0.01).

Figure 15:Extrapolating ARC Challenge accuracy along the pretraining trajectory of OLMo-3-7B.Circles are the training window (up to8080K steps), the star is the held-out checkpoint at1.41.4M steps (∼18×{\sim}18\timesthe training compute).Left:accuracy vs.log10⁡(steps)\log_{10}(\text{steps}), fit with a sigmoid (RMSE=0.07=0.07).Centre:accuracy vs. CE loss on FineWeb, fit with an exponential (RMSE=0.13=0.13).Right:accuracy vs. the best univariate proxy, fit with a power law (RMSE=0.02=0.02).
